title: Estimating Unknown Work
description: Three-point estimation (Pessimistic/Optimistic/Most Likely), Cone of Uncertainty, confidence intervals, communicating estimates to leadership, Staff+ estimation scenarios (migration scoping, platform build).

description.value:
Three-point estimation is the most widely used technique at FAANG for estimating engineering work. Instead of giving a single number ("this will take 3 months"), you give three numbers: Optimistic (O), Most Likely (M), and Pessimistic (P). This acknowledges uncertainty and produces a statistically meaningful estimate.

The Three Numbers: Optimistic (O) — everything goes perfectly. No unexpected dependencies, no staffing delays, no scope creep, all technologies work as advertised. This is the best-case scenario, not the unrealistic "if we work weekends" scenario. Most Likely (M) — based on your experience with similar projects, including typical delays, minor scope changes, and normal team velocity. This is your honest best guess. Pessimistic (P) — everything that can go wrong does go wrong. Key team member leaves, major architecture change required, integration partner delays by 2 months, compliance review takes twice as long.

Calculating Expected Time: The expected time (E) using the beta distribution (PERT method): E = (O + 4*M + P) / 6. This gives more weight to the Most Likely estimate while still accounting for tail risks. The standard deviation: SD = (P - O) / 6. This gives you the confidence interval: there is a 68% probability that the actual time falls within E ± SD, and 95% probability within E ± 2*SD.

Example — S/4 Custom Code Assessment: O = 3 weeks (existing tools cover 80% of analysis, team is fully staffed). M = 6 weeks (some manual analysis needed, one dependency delays by a week). P = 12 weeks (tooling doesn't cover legacy Z-programs, two team members pulled to production support). Expected: (3 + 4*6 + 12) / 6 = 6.5 weeks. Standard deviation: (12 - 3) / 6 = 1.5 weeks. 95% confidence interval: 6.5 ± 3 = 3.5 to 9.5 weeks. When you communicate this to leadership, you say: "We estimate 6.5 weeks with a range of 4 to 9 weeks at 95% confidence." This is much more honest and useful than saying "6 weeks."

decisionTree.title: Choosing the Right Estimation Method
decisionTree.prompt: Should I use three-point PERT, analogous estimation, or parametric modeling for this project?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "How much historical data do I have from similar projects?" If you have 5+ similar completed projects with tracked durations, use analogous estimation. If you have 0-2 data points or high uncertainty, use three-point PERT. If the work decomposes into repeatable units (e.g., per-interface migration effort), use parametric.

branches.condition: 5+ historical data points from similar projects available
branches.path: Analogous estimation. Compare scope, complexity, and team size to past projects. Adjust for differences. Most accurate when projects are truly similar (same domain, same team, same tech stack).

branches.condition: 0-2 data points, high uncertainty, or unique project
branches.path: Three-point PERT. Optimistic / Most Likely / Pessimistic with beta distribution. Best for first-of-its-kind projects like an S/4 migration or a new integration hub.

branches.condition: Work decomposes into repeatable units with known per-unit cost
branches.path: Parametric estimation. Count units × per-unit effort. Example: '200 interfaces × 2 weeks each = 400 weeks of migration effort.' Adjust for complexity variance. Best for large-scale migrations with many similar items.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The best Staff+ answer combines methods: 'I use parametric for the base estimate (200 interfaces × 2 weeks = 400 weeks), then three-point PERT for the uncertainty range (±30% due to discovery risk), and analogous to validate against our last S/4 migration.'

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Estimation Precision vs Speed of Delivery

variables.name: Discovery phase duration
variables.source: S/4 assessment pilot: 2 weeks for tooling, 2 more if gaps found

cp.monthlyPenalty: Invest 2 weeks in discovery: reduces estimate range from ±50% to ±20%. Cost: 2 weeks of senior engineer time ($15K). Benefit: leadership can make confident staffing decisions, avoiding $500K of over-hiring or under-hiring risk.

ap.monthlyPenalty: Skip discovery, estimate from experience: faster start but ±50% range. Cost: none upfront. Risk: estimate may be off by 3+ months, causing missed deadlines or overstaffing costs of $200K+.

calculation.breakeven: If the project is >$1M total cost, discovery pays for itself 10x over by reducing estimation error. For projects under $200K, the discovery cost may exceed the benefit of reduced uncertainty.

description.value:
The Cone of Uncertainty (first described by Steve McConnell) describes how estimation accuracy improves as a project progresses. At the initial concept phase, the estimate could be off by a factor of 4x (high-level estimate). At the requirements complete phase, it narrows to 2x. At design complete, 1.5x. At coding complete, 1.25x. This is not an excuse for bad estimates — it's a tool for communicating why estimates change and for calibrating stakeholder expectations.

Why Uncertainty is High in SAP Projects: SAP projects have inherent uncertainty that general software projects don't. (1) System Discovery — you don't know the full extent of customizations, modified standards, and technical debt until you start the assessment. A single custom BADI can cascade into weeks of remediation work. (2) Integration Complexity — you don't know all the interfaces until you find them. SAP systems have hundreds of RFC destinations, IDoc receivers, and PI/PO channels that may be undocumented. (3) Data Quality — you don't know the data quality until you analyze it. Bad master data can block an entire migration. (4) Organizational Dependencies — you don't know which business stakeholders will block or delay decisions until the project is underway.

How to Use the Cone: At each phase, re-estimate and communicate the new range. At the Discovery phase: "Our estimate is 6–12 months for the full migration." At Design Complete: "Our estimate is now 8–10 months." At Coding Complete: "We're on track for 9 months, ± 2 weeks." The narrowing cone shows that uncertainty is being resolved and the estimate is converging. If the estimate doesn't converge (e.g., 6–12 months at design complete), that's a red flag — uncertainty is not being resolved.

class="cn-box" x="20" y="130" width="640" height="100"/><text class="cn-lbl" x="340" y="150">SAP-Specific Uncertainty Drivers</text><text class="cn-sub" x="340" y="168">System Discovery: Unknown customizations, modified SAP standard objects</text><text class="cn-sub" x="340" y="184">Integration Complexity: Undocumented RFC destinations, IDoc flows</text><text class="cn-sub" x="340" y="200">Data Quality: Bad master data blocking migration | Org dependencies: Stakeholder delays</text><rect class="cn-box" x="20" y="245" width="640" height="80"/><text class="cn-lbl" x="340" y="263">How to Communicate Using the Cone</text><text class="cn-sub" x="340" y="281">At every phase: re-estimate and show the narrowing range</text><text class="cn-sub" x="340" y="297">If the range doesn't narrow — that's a red flag (uncertainty not being resolved)</text><text class="cn-sub" x="340" y="313">Leaders trust engineers who acknowledge uncertainty and track its resolution</text></svg>

A single-point estimate ("6 weeks") is almost always wrong. A confidence interval ("4 to 9 weeks with 95% confidence") acknowledges uncertainty and builds trust. The key skill is communicating the confidence interval in a way that stakeholders can act on without being paralyzed by the range.

Choosing the Confidence Level: For different decisions, different confidence levels are appropriate. For budget allocation ("should we hire 2 more contractors?"): 80% confidence interval is sufficient — you want to be reasonably sure but don't need extreme precision. For go-live scheduling ("should we announce a go-live date to the board?"): 95% confidence is required — the cost of being wrong is high. For compliance deadlines ("we must be S/4-ready by Dec 2027"): 99% confidence — missing a regulatory deadline is unacceptable.

Decomposing Uncertainty: A vague range ("4 to 12 weeks") is not helpful. Decompose uncertainty by category. "The estimate is 6.5 weeks ± 2.5 weeks. The uncertainty breaks down as: tooling discovery (30% of uncertainty), integration mapping (25%), team availability (20%), stakeholder review cycles (15%), and external dependencies (10%)." This helps stakeholders understand where the risk is and what can be done to reduce it. If management wants a firmer estimate, they can invest in tooling discovery first (reducing 30% of uncertainty).

The Estimate Deceit: There's a toxic pattern in SAP projects where engineers give a single-point estimate they know is wrong because that's what management wants to hear. The project then runs late, and everyone acts surprised. Staff+ engineers have a responsibility to break this pattern by giving honest ranges. If a VP asks "When will the migration be done?" and you say "9 months ± 3 months," you might be disliked for not giving a precise date. But you're respected for being honest. And when the estimate converges to 10 months, you're seen as credible, not incompetent.

organizationalDimension.title: Building a Culture of Honest Estimation
organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: The 'Estimation Transparency' framework: Phase 1 introduces three-point estimation as a team practice — every sprint, teams give O/M/P for their committed work. Phase 2 creates a historical database of estimates vs actuals to calibrate future estimates. Phase 3 extends the practice to cross-team initiatives. Phase 4 (12 months) makes confidence intervals the default communication format to leadership.

stakeholders.concern: Ranges sound uncertain — leadership prefers single dates
stakeholders.address: Training session: show how single-point estimates create false precision and erode trust when missed. Ranges build trust because they acknowledge reality. Provide a script: 'The range is 6-9 months. At month 6, we will reconfirm.'

stakeholders.concern: Giving pessimistic estimates makes us look slow
stakeholders.address: Track estimate accuracy (actual vs predicted) as a team metric. Teams that give honest ranges have better accuracy. The PERT formula already weights most-likely higher than pessimistic — the range is calibrated, not conservative.

stakeholders.concern: The board expects firm dates, not ranges
stakeholders.address: Give the board a commitment date (P95) and a target date (P50). The commitment date is the promise. The target date is what the team is working toward. If the board asks for a single date, give the commitment date and explain the buffer.

organizationalDimension.adoptionPhases: Phase 1 (sprint-level): O/M/P for every commitment. Phase 2 (monthly): calibrate estimates against historical data. Phase 3 (quarterly): cross-team estimation with confidence intervals. Phase 4 (annual): leadership communication in ranges.
organizationalDimension.metrics: Month 3: 80% of teams use three-point estimation. Month 6: estimate accuracy improved from ±60% to ±30%. Month 12: all leadership reporting uses confidence intervals.
organizationalDimension.antiPatterns: (1) Pessimistic estimate is treated as the 'real' estimate — undermines the PERT methodology. (2) No historical calibration — estimates don't improve because no one tracks actuals vs predictions. (3) Ranges used to CYA instead of communicate — '2-20 weeks' is not helpful.

description.value:
Communicating estimates to leadership is a relational skill, not just a mathematical one. Leaders don't care about PERT formulas or confidence intervals — they care about: (1) Can we make the deadline? (2) If not, what can we do about it? (3) How much will it cost? (4) What's the risk? Your job is to translate engineering uncertainty into business language.

The Three-Number Communication: For any estimate, communicate three numbers: Commitment Date (the date you are 95% confident you can deliver by — this is your promise), Target Date (the date you're working toward, approximately P50 — this is the goal), and Best Case Date (if everything goes perfectly — this is the aspiration, not the plan). Example: "We are committing to deliver by Dec 15 (95% confidence). We're targeting Nov 30. Our stretch goal is Nov 15. The main driver of the range is the integration discovery phase, which we're starting next week."

Tradeoff Conversations: When a leader says "The deadline is fixed, make it work," you escalate to the tradeoff conversation: "We can deliver by the fixed deadline if we reduce scope, add resources, or accept higher risk. Which tradeoff would you like to make?" This is the triple constraint: scope, resources, time — you can fix two, but not all three. Example: "To deliver by Oct 1, we'd need to reduce the custom code migration scope from 200 programs to 100 (scope reduction), or add 3 more developers (resource addition), or accept a 20% failure rate on the first cutover (risk acceptance)."

Estimation as Negotiation: Estimation is not a one-way communication. It's a negotiation where you educate leadership about uncertainty and they educate you about business constraints. The best outcome is a shared understanding: "We understand the regulatory deadline is Dec 2027. Given this constraint, we recommend starting the discovery phase immediately and adding 2 contractors for the assessment. Our estimate will converge to a firmer number by end of Q1." This positions you as a partner in solving the business problem, not an obstructionist who won't commit to a date.

and add</text><text class="cl-sub" x="520" y="293">2 contractors. Estimate converges by Q1."</text></svg>

Here are two Staff+ estimation scenarios that commonly appear in FAANG interviews. Practice these with three-point estimation and confidence intervals.

Scenario 1: Migration Scoping (S/4HANA Custom Code Assessment). We need to estimate the effort to assess 3,200 Z-programs for S/4HANA compatibility. The assessment involves: automated static analysis (SAP Readiness Check 2.0), manual review of custom BADIs and modified SAP standard objects, and categorization by remediation effort (compatible, minor change, major rewrite, deprecate). Three-point estimate: O = 3 weeks (if Readiness Check covers 90% of analysis, only 10% manual review needed). M = 6 weeks (typical coverage 70%, 30% manual review). P = 12 weeks (tooling gaps require full manual review, team pulled to support). Expected: 6.5 weeks, SD: 1.5 weeks. 95% CI: 3.5–9.5 weeks. The key uncertainty is tooling coverage — invest 2 weeks in a pilot assessment first to narrow the range.

Scenario 2: Platform Build (Integration Hub on Event Mesh). We need to estimate building the integration hub platform: Event Mesh cluster setup, connector development for SAP and non-SAP systems, schema registry, monitoring dashboards, and migration of 100 priority interfaces from PI/PO. Three-point estimate: O = 4 months (existing team has Event Mesh experience, reusable connector library covers 60% of needs, 20 interfaces/month migration velocity). M = 7 months (new team learning curve, 50% reusable connectors, 15 interfaces/month). P = 14 months (PI/PO migration reveals undocumented customizations in 40% of interfaces, 2 team members leave mid-project, compliance review adds 2 months). Expected: (4 + 4*7 + 14) / 6 = 7.7 months. SD: (14 - 4) / 6 = 1.7 months. 95% CI: 4.3–11 months. Key risk: PI/PO interface discovery — start with a 2-week discovery sprint before committing to the estimate.

coverage</text><text class="ss-sub" x="175" y="147">Mitigation: 2-week pilot assessment</text><text class="ss-sub" x="175" y="163">3,200 Z-programs to assess</text><rect class="ss-scen" x="370" y="55" width="310" height="130" stroke="var(--brand-primary)"/><text class="ss-lbl" x="525" y="75">Scenario 2: Integration Hub Build</text><text class="ss-sub" x="525" y="95">O=4m, M=7m, P=14m → E=7.7m, SD=1.7m</text><text class="ss-sub" x="525" y="113">95% CI: 4.3–11 months</text><text class="ss-sub" x="525" y="131">Key risk: PI/PO discovery</text><text class="ss-sub" x="525" y="147">Mitigation: 2-week discovery sprint</text><text class="ss-sub" x="525" y="163">Migration of 100 interfaces</text><rect class="ss-box" x="20" y="205" width="640" height="120"/><text class="ss-lbl" x="340" y="225">Interview Practice: Estimate an Unknown Project</text><text class="ss-sub" x="340" y="245">When asked: "How long would it take to build X?" → DON'T give a single number</text><text class="ss-sub" x="340" y="263">DO: "Here's my three-point estimate. The main uncertainty is [factor].</text><text class="ss-sub" x="340" y="281">If we invest in [mitigation], we can narrow the range."</text><text class="ss-sub" x="340" y="299">This shows: structured thinking, uncertainty awareness, and risk management — all Staff+ signals.</text></svg>

adversarialResponse.title: Handling 'Your estimate was off by 50%. Why should we trust you?'
adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your estimate was 6-12 months. The project took 14 months. You missed by 16-133%. Why should we trust your next estimate?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "The scope changed after the estimate was given. It was not my fault." (defensive, blames external factors, no learning)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You are right to flag that. The estimate was wrong, and I need to own that. Let me analyze why. The primary driver was the interface discovery gap — we found 40% more interfaces than anticipated during the migration, which was not captured in the initial assessment. The root cause: I estimated based on the documented interface inventory, but 40% of interfaces were undocumented. The corrective action for future estimates: I now require a minimum 2-week discovery sprint before any migration estimate, specifically including a network scan to identify undocumented interfaces. I also add a 30% 'unknown discovery' buffer to any estimate where the discovery phase is incomplete. The lesson: any estimate that depends on 'what we know about the system' must account for 'what we don't know we don't know.' I now include an explicit 'unknown unknowns' adjustment factor in my estimation framework."
adversarialResponse.pattern: own-analyze-correct — own the miss without excuses, identify the specific root cause (undocumented interfaces), describe the process change (discovery sprint + unknown unknowns buffer), and show the systemic lesson. The Staff+ signal is not being right — it's learning from being wrong and institutionalizing the fix.

details: Staff+ Insight: Estimation is not about being right — it's about managing uncertainty. The ability to give a three-point estimate with confidence intervals, decompose uncertainty by category, and communicate ranges to leadership is a core Staff+ competency. In FAANG interviews, estimation questions test your ability to think structurally about unknowns. Never give a single-point estimate. Always frame your answer around the cone of uncertainty and the confidence level appropriate for the decision being made.
